opp.ais_why:
• Elie Sauma described AI-first leadership on the 4/9/2026 call: leadership has embraced AI-driven automation across procurement, and his team has already built in-house automation using Microsoft Forms, Lists, Power Automate, and the Coupa API at zero spend. They've deployed two Copilot agents covering procurement and travel inquiries, and they plan to import Zycus training materials into that Copilot environment.
• Sitecore asked Zycus to dig into the AP side, specifically AI invoice scanning, and noted that their SVP has committed to automating AP invoice processing to replace manual email scanning into Coupa. After Hemant walked through AP Smartdesk, Elie's team flagged interest in AI-enabled invoice extraction to auto-populate eInvoice fields, again buyer-pulled.
• They also stated a requirement for AI-driven ESG risk monitoring across anti-slavery, anti-bribery, and supplier compliance, and they've built an intake system inside Microsoft Teams, which maps directly to Merlin Intake on Teams. With 90%+ Azure infrastructure, a formal RFP planned, and demo scheduling moving inside a week, this is a textbook AI Hungry profile.

metrics.narrative: The business problem is clear and quantified in places but a formal ROI/value case is not yet built. Elie Sauma stated the goal is functional, not financial: on 15 May 2026 he said 'I'm not looking to save money. I'm just looking to replace Coupa, and if we're all within the budget we are today, I'm okay.' The AP pain is concrete: on the 9 April 2026 call Elie said Invoice Smash is processing 'only 5% or 10% of our invoices' with the rest handled manually via AP mailboxes, and Coupa 'admitted it's not working' yet gave no credit. The metric gap is that no dollar savings, cycle-time, or headcount target has been agreed, and Elie deferred budget ('I can't talk about that right now', 15 May). Quantifying the AP automation impact and total cost of ownership including transition cost (which Elie flagged as the real variable for his economic buyer) is the open move.

champion.narrative: Elie Sauma, VP/Head of Procurement & Travel, is an engaged, accessible champion and the deal arrived through a trusted reference. On 9 April 2026 he disclosed his personal trust network: he worked for the Paul Hastings CPO (a Zycus advocate) at two prior companies, 'she's a good friend of mine... for her to go with Zycus running an RFP tells me a lot', and called Zycus 'automatically a finalist.' He is transparent about the process, gives candid coaching (e.g. the 'too much in the demo' feedback on 15 May), and proactively confirmed finalist status. The development still needed: Elie is procurement, not the economic buyer, and is currently the single thread. His advocacy is strong but must be converted into access to the finance approver and the AP end-user team. Current engagement is active (last buyer reply 24 Jun 2026, 6 days ago).

competition.narrative: Coupa is the incumbent being actively displaced after 5-6 years, and the buyer's sentiment toward it is strongly negative: Elie Sauma (9 April 2026) cited failed Invoice Smash, no credit/support, and post-Thoma Bravo decline in service and AI innovation. Zycus is a confirmed finalist of four (Elie, 15 May 2026: 'I expect you guys to be definitely one of the 4 finalists') and benefits from a trusted reference and a value-over-price selection bias that favours its unified Merlin platform. The gap is intelligence on the other three RFP finalists: their identities, positioning, and pricing are unknown, and the RFP (responses due 30 June) will score against them. Relich appears only as a third-party ESG/supplier-risk tool integrated with Coupa, treated as a capability gap rather than a competing platform. Winning requires leading on AP automation fit, the Microsoft/Teams-native AI intake story, and a defensible total cost (including transition) within Coupa's current budget.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific, owned, and confirmed by the buyer. Primary pain is failed AP automation: Elie Sauma (9 April 2026) said Invoice Smash 'has just not worked for us as a company' and processes 'only 5% or 10%' of invoices, leaving the rest manual via AP mailboxes; the senior VP of accounting 'is 100% looking at automating that process.' Secondary pain is Coupa's decline post-Thoma Bravo acquisition: declining customer service, no AI innovation, and modules they pay for but do not use (CLM). Elie also flagged a known gap in supplier risk/ESG (anti-slavery, anti-bribery) where they currently bolt on Relich. The pain owners are Elie (procurement) and the SVP of Accounting (AP automation). This pain set directly motivates the Coupa replacement and aligns to the relevant stakeholder, Merlin CLM, and supplier-risk modules.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are partially visible. The RFP is the active paper artifact (responses due 30 June EOD per Next Step). NDA was discussed but deferred on 9 April 2026: Elie said 'we'll wait a little bit... once we go through these first things, you know, we can send you an NDA', and confirmed it is 'one NDA that's pretty industry standard.' The forcing function is the Coupa renewal: Elie noted on 9 April 'we just renewed for a year, so that's why I wanted to kick this off right away so that... we have enough time to do it', targeting transition August 2026-March 2027 ahead of the next renewal. There is no visibility yet into MSA/legal review, procurement signature authority, or InfoSec. Confirming the contracting and legal path (and who signs) once shortlist position is secured would create paper-process visibility.

economic_buyer.narrative: The final budget approver has not been named, introduced, or engaged. Elie Sauma controls the procurement evaluation and is the decision driver, but on 15 May 2026 he made clear the commercial decision goes higher: 'I would like to be able to present back a business case in mid July latest... and get a decision by July', and earlier (9 April 2026) he referenced building the business case 'internally with our CFO because that... can take some time.' He also noted private-equity ownership tightened budgets ('they stepped in here a year ago. So things are super tight there'), signalling cost discipline at the approval level. So the evidence shows a finance approver exists above Elie and will receive the business case in mid-July, but that person is unmapped, unquoted, and unengaged. Identifying and getting a Zycus narrative in front of the economic buyer before the mid-July business case is the highest-value gap to close.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer is running a structured, self-managed RFP and Elie Sauma has laid the process out explicitly. From the 15 May 2026 call and Next Step: meet the last vendor, down-select to 4 finalists, issue the RFP by mid-June (preference early June), RFP responses due 30 June EOD, evaluate early July, present a business case to the economic buyer mid-July, decision end of July, then transition August 2026-March 2027. The call sequence corroborates this: intro (9 Apr) with Elie + Harsh, first demo (30 Apr) adding Anson on the AP/systems side, then Elie's pricing/timeline check (15 May). Elie owns the RFP and declined help drafting it ('I have that... we've me and her have done this before', 9 April). The approval chain runs procurement (Elie) up to a finance approver for the business case. The process is clear; the gap is Zycus access to the finance approval step.

sources: Avoma intro call 9 Apr 2026 (Elie Sauma, Mike McCarthy)
decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well-articulated. Elie Sauma framed the decision on 15 May 2026 as best-fit-within-budget, not cheapest: 'I'm gonna go with the best tool possible within my budget... the best fit for us within the budget. That that's the decision we're gonna make.' Functionally, the criteria centre on (1) AP/invoice automation to replace failing Invoice Smash, (2) AI/agentic capability (Elie on 9 April: leadership 'has really taken on the AI challenge to the entire company'), (3) Microsoft/Teams-native intake and Azure fit ('we are very, very close strategic partnership with Microsoft'), and (4) smooth Coupa migration. For the follow-up demo Elie specified the exact focus on 15 May: vendor onboarding, OFAC screening, and AP workflows for the finance teams, with the RFP carrying written demo guidelines. These are confirmed, buyer-stated criteria that favour Zycus's unified Merlin platform story.

items.detail: RFP bake-off with 3 unknown competitors and no clear lead position yet. Zycus is confirmed as one of 4 finalists (15 May) but has not differentiated enough to be the sole front-runner. The buyer's selection is best-fit within budget (not cheapest), which advantages Zycus's unified AI platform story, but the competitive field is unknown and the RFP scoring (responses due 30 June, 4 days out) will determine the outcome. Risk: a competitor with a more surgical RFP response, a stronger CFO business case narrative, or a lower-price bid could win if Zycus does not lead on fit and value.
items.status: Active. RFP responses due 30 June (4 days out). Mitigation: deliver a focused RFP response on Sitecore's pain points (AP automation, Microsoft integration, unified platform) and co-author the CFO business case to embed differentiation into the internal sell.

items.detail: The Salesforce close date (30 July, 34 days out) is ahead of the buyer's realistic decision and contracting timeline. Elie's stated timeline (15 May call) is: RFP responses due 30 June, evaluation early July, business case to CFO mid-July, decision end of July, then contract negotiation and transition August-March 2027. That timeline implies contract close in August, not 30 July. If Zycus forecasts to 30 July and the buyer's process runs to its natural pace, the deal will slip 2-4 weeks, creating internal forecast miss and pressure on the deal team.
items.status: Active. Buyer timeline (decision end July, contract close August) diverges from Salesforce close date (30 July). Mitigation: align internal forecast to August and work with the buyer to accelerate CFO approval and contracting if possible, but do not push the buyer to compress a well-structured process.

items.detail: No CFO engagement yet, and the CFO is the final budget approver. Elie will present the business case to the CFO by mid-July (target ~15 July, 19 days out), but Zycus has not been introduced to the CFO, has not shaped the business case narrative, and does not know the CFO's decision criteria or risk concerns. If the business case is built in isolation, it may default to a price-driven comparison or miss the CFO's priorities (e.g. PE cost discipline, ROI proof, implementation risk). Risk: the CFO rejects or delays the decision, or chooses a lower-cost competitor, because Zycus's value story was not embedded in the business case.
items.status: Active. CFO business case presentation targeted mid-July (19 days out); Zycus not yet engaged in shaping it. Mitigation: proactively offer to co-author the ROI model and business case with Elie, request a CFO intro or joint session to align on value, and provide Paul Hastings reference data to de-risk the decision.

items.detail: Migration complexity from a mature 5-6 year Coupa deployment is a stated buyer concern. Elie said (9 April): 'the ease of doing that, we're really gonna need to convince people, especially leadership. I think it's the right time.' Sitecore has a well-established P2P process with users accustomed to Coupa, and leadership buy-in for the migration (which includes budget impact, implementation timeline, and change management load) is required. If migration plan is not detailed and de-risked, leadership may defer the decision or choose to renew Coupa for another year.
items.status: Acknowledged but not yet mitigated. Recommended move: after RFP decision (early-mid July), immediately kick off joint implementation planning session with Elie, Harsh, Anson, and Zycus implementation lead to de-risk migration complexity and document SOW scope before CFO business case presentation mid-July.

items.detail: Single-threaded to Elie Sauma (procurement) with no direct engagement from CFO, SVP of Accounting, or AP/finance team. items.status: Unmitigated. He is looking for best fit for their needs and not necessarily a cheaper solution.' This sets a ceiling: Zycus must match or beat Coupa's pricing. A competitor with a lower-price bid could win if Zycus does not lead on fit and value. RFP scoring (early July) will likely include a price component. Zycus's positioning is value-over-price (unified AI platform, Merlin differentiation), but CFO's final decision may default to price if ROI case is not compelling.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 9 Apr 2026, ~82 days old, in Shortlisted. Close 30 Jul 2026 = 30 days out. Last buyer touch 24 Jun 2026 (incoming reply, 6 days ago); RFP response due 30 Jun EOD (today). Elie's stated path (15 May): RFP responses due 30 Jun, evaluation early Jul, business case to economic buyer mid-Jul, decision end Jul, then negotiation + transition Aug 2026-Mar 2027. Decision end of July leaves no room for negotiation and signature by 30 Jul, so contract execution realistically lands early-mid August. Verdict is timing-only; the deal itself is live and progressing.
evidence: Elie Sauma, 15 May 2026 call: 'I really want us to roll out the RFP at the latest mid June... I would like to be able to present back a business case in mid July latest... get a decision by July and then... August time frame till March next year to kind of work on transition.'
Next Step (SF): '6/29 - Zycus submitted our Vendor questions for the RFP - Our response to their RFP is due 6/30 EOD'.
Elie Sauma, 15 May 2026 call: 'I expect you guys to be definitely one of the 4 finalists.'
north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy, engaged RFP finalist; the 30 July close date is ~2-4 weeks ahead of Elie's own decision-then-contract timeline, so the date will slip while the deal stays strong.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Best Case / Upside (decision end July, contract August) rather than a 30 July Commit; the economic buyer is unmapped and contracting falls after the close date.

flags: Co-author the CFO business case with Elie before the mid-July presentation. The CFO is the final budget approver, unmapped and unengaged, and will receive Elie's business case recommendation by mid-July (~15 July, 15 days out). If the business case is built in isolation it may default to a price-driven comparison or miss the CFO's priorities (PE cost discipline, ROI proof, migration risk). Offer to provide Elie with: (1) AP automation ROI calculator (quantify manual entry elimination for 9,000+ invoices/month, duplicate-pay savings via the relevant stakeholder, faster close cycle time), (2) reference customer case study and ROI data, (3) migration risk mitigation plan (timeline, resource commitment, Zycus-on-Zycus implementation model). Position as 'help you build a stronger CFO business case with our data.
Deliver a focused RFP response (due 30 June, 4 days out) that leads on the buyer's top priorities: AP automation, Microsoft integration, unified AI platform, and proven migration

Single-threaded to Elie Sauma (procurement). Elie is driving the RFP evaluation and is aligned on the need and timeline, but he is the evaluator and recommender, not a champion with EB access who is advocating for Zycus internally. No evidence of coaching behaviors (e.g., sharing internal competitive intel, introducing Zycus to the CFO, mobilizing other stakeholders). Harsh Sejpal and Anson Malanakarot are engaged but are junior stakeholders without decision authority. Risk: if Elie is the only internal advocate and he does not escalate Zycus to the CFO or mobilize AP/finance buy-in, the deal can stall or lose to a competitor who secures a stronger internal champion. Recommended move: convert Elie into a coach by asking him how Zycus should position to the CFO and what the CFO's top concerns are, and request an introduction to the SVP of Accounting or CFO before the business case presentation.

Three unknown competitors in the RFP shortlist. Elie confirmed Zycus is one of 4 finalists (15 May per Next Step), but the identity, positioning, and strengths of the other 3 competitors are unknown. A competitor with a stronger RFP response, a more surgical AP automation story, or a lower-price bid could win if Zycus does not differentiate on fit and value. The RFP scoring (responses due 30 June, evaluation early July) will determine shortlist position. Recommended move: after RFP submission (30 June), request a competitive readout from Elie—how did Zycus score vs the other 3 finalists, and what are their perceived strengths—so Zycus can adjust positioning before the CFO business case.

Timeline misalignment creates a 2-4 week slip risk. The Salesforce close date is 30 July (31 days out), but the buyer's stated timeline (Elie, 15 May per Next Step: 'RFP responses due 30 June, business case mid-July, decision end of July') implies contract execution in early August, not 30 July. If Zycus forecasts to 30 July and the buyer's process runs to its natural pace (decision end of July, then contract negotiation and MSA/SOW review), the deal will slip 2-4 weeks, creating internal forecast miss and pressure on the deal team. Recommended move: after the decision (end of July), immediately kick off MSA/SOW review, InfoSec questionnaire, and ERP integration scoping in parallel to compress contracting timeline and avoid an August slip.

Discover the other 3 RFP finalists and their positioning. Zycus is one of 4 finalists but has zero intelligence on the other 3 competitors (names, strengths, buyer perception). This is the deal's largest blind spot. In the early July check-in or follow-up demo scheduling conversation, ask Elie or Harsh: 'To tailor our follow-up demo and business case to what matters most to you, can you share who the other finalists are and what you see as their strengths vs Zycus?' Without this, Zycus cannot differentiate surgically in the RFP scoring or CFO business case.
best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers dominate at this stage: nail the RFP response due today, get a Zycus narrative into the mid-July business case the economic buyer will see, and convert single-threading by engaging the AP/finance end users.

hard.ais_why:
• Elie Sauma described AI-first leadership on the 4/9/2026 call: leadership has embraced AI-driven automation across procurement, and his team has already built in-house automation using Microsoft Forms, Lists, Power Automate, and the Coupa API at zero spend. They've deployed two Copilot agents covering procurement and travel inquiries, and they plan to import Zycus training materials into that Copilot environment.
• Sitecore asked Zycus to dig into the AP side, specifically AI invoice scanning, and noted that their SVP has committed to automating AP invoice processing to replace manual email scanning into Coupa. After Hemant walked through AP Smartdesk, Elie's team flagged interest in AI-enabled invoice extraction to auto-populate eInvoice fields, again buyer-pulled.
• They also stated a requirement for AI-driven ESG risk monitoring across anti-slavery, anti-bribery, and supplier compliance, and they've built an intake system inside Microsoft Teams, which maps directly to Merlin Intake on Teams. With 90%+ Azure infrastructure, a formal RFP planned, and demo scheduling moving inside a week, this is a textbook AI Hungry profile.

deltas.from: Migration complexity from a mature 5-6 year Coupa deployment (P2P, VIM, CLM partially used) is a stated concern. Elie said 'the ease of doing that… we're really
deltas.subject: Migration complexity from a mature 5-6 year Coupa deployment (multi-module: P2P, VIM, CLM partially used) is a stated buyer concern. Elie said on the 9 April call: 'the ease of doing that … we're really gonna need to kind of convince people, especially leadership. I think it's the right time.' Sitecore has a well-established P2P process with users accustomed to Coupa, and leadership buy-in for the migration (which includes budget impact, implementation timeline, and change management load) is required. If the migration plan is not detailed and de-risked, leadership may defer the decision or choose to renew Coupa for another year. Risk: the deal stalls on 'let's wait another year' or 'the migration is too disruptive.'

deltas.subject: Single-threaded to Elie Sauma (procurement) with no direct engagement from the CFO, SVP of Accounting, or AP/finance team. The AP team owns the Invoice Smash pain and will be end-users of the solution, but they have not been on a call (the 30 April demo included Harsh and Anson, both procurement/systems, not AP). The SVP of Accounting 'is 100% looking at automating that process' (Elie, 9 April) but has not been engaged. If the AP stakeholders are not convinced, they can veto or delay the decision. Risk: the deal is approved by procurement but rejected or deprioritized by finance/AP, or the AP team prefers a competitor's solution after the follow-up demo.

deltas.subject: Pricing must be comparable to Coupa's current spend to stay competitive. Elie stated 'as long as pricing is comparable to current spend with Coupa, that's fine. He is looking for best fit for their needs and not necessarily a cheaper solution' (15 May per Next Step). This sets a ceiling: Zycus must match or beat Coupa's pricing, and a competitor with a lower-price bid could win if Zycus does not lead on fit and value. The RFP scoring (responses due 30 June, evaluation early July) will likely include a price component. Zycus's positioning is value-over-price (unified AI platform, Merlin differentiation), but the CFO's final decision may default to price if the ROI case is not compelling.

deltas.subject: Discover the other 3 RFP finalists and their positioning. Zycus is one of 4 finalists but has zero intelligence on the other 3 competitors (names, strengths, buyer perception). This is the deal's largest blind spot. In the early July check-in or follow-up demo scheduling conversation, ask Elie or Harsh: 'To tailor our follow-up demo and business case to what matters most to you, can you share who the other finalists are and what you see as their strengths vs Zycus?' Without this, Zycus cannot differentiate surgically in the RFP scoring or CFO business case.

deltas.to: Co-author the CFO business case with Elie before the mid-July presentation. The CFO is the final budget approver, unmapped and unengaged, and will receive Elie'
deltas.subject: Co-author the CFO business case with Elie before the mid-July presentation. The CFO is the final budget approver, unmapped and unengaged, and will receive Elie's business case recommendation by mid-July (~15 July, 15 days out). If the business case is built in isolation it may default to a price-driven comparison or miss the CFO's priorities (PE cost discipline, ROI proof, migration risk). Offer to provide Elie with: (1) AP automation ROI calculator (quantify manual entry elimination for 9,000+ invoices/month, duplicate-pay savings via the relevant stakeholder, faster close cycle time), (2) reference customer case study and ROI data, (3) migration risk mitigation plan (timeline, resource commitment, Zycus-on-Zycus implementation model). Position as 'help you build a stronger CFO business case with our data.

deltas.subject: Multi-thread to CFO and SVP of Accounting before the decision. Deal is currently single-threaded to Elie (procurement). The CFO is the final approver (unmapped, will receive business case mid-July). The SVP of Accounting owns the AP pain (10,000 invoices/month, Invoice Smash failure) and is '100% looking at automating that process' per Elie but has not been engaged. Request Elie introduce Zycus to the CFO for a 20-minute ROI/migration risk discussion before mid-July, and schedule the follow-up demo for the SVP of Accounting and AP team (focus on AP Smartdesk invoice intelligence, duplicate-pay detection, vendor onboarding/OFAC per Elie's stated demo guidelines). Multi-threading before the decision reduces single-point-of-failure risk and embeds Zycus's value story directly with the two power roles (CFO budget approval, SVP of Accounting AP pain ownership).

Elie's stated timeline (15 May call) is: RFP responses due 30 June, evaluation early July, business case to CFO mid-July, decision end of July, then contract negotiation and transition August-March 2027. deltas.subject: No CFO engagement yet, and the CFO is the final budget approver. Elie will present the business case to the CFO by mid-July (target ~15 July, 19 days out), but Zycus has not been introduced to the CFO, has not shaped the business case narrative, and does not know the CFO's decision criteria or risk concerns. If the business case is built in isolation, it may default to a price-driven comparison or miss the CFO's priorities (e.g. PE cost discipline, ROI proof, implementation risk). Risk: the CFO rejects or delays the decision, or chooses a lower-cost competitor, because Zycus's value story was not embedded in the business case.

deltas.subject: Migration complexity from a mature 5-6 year Coupa deployment (multi-module: P2P, VIM, CLM partially used) is a stated buyer concern. Elie said on the 9 April call: 'the ease of doing that … we're really gonna need to kind of convince people, especially leadership. I think it's the right time.' Sitecore has a well-established P2P process with users accustomed to Coupa, and leadership buy-in for the migration (which includes budget impact, implementation timeline, and change management load) is required. If the migration plan is not detailed and de-risked, leadership may defer the decision or choose to renew Coupa for another year. Risk: the deal stalls on 'let's wait another year' or 'the migration is too disruptive.'
